sections.heading: Inheritance
sections.content: Inheritance allows a class (subclass) to inherit properties and methods from another class (parent or superclass).

sections.heading: The open Keyword
sections.content: In Kotlin, classes are final by default. You must explicitly mark a class as open to allow inheritance.

sections.heading: Overriding Methods
sections.content: To allow subclasses to modify behavior, the parent method must be marked open, and the subclass must use the override keyword.

sections.heading: Interfaces
sections.content: An interface defines a contract of properties and methods that a class must implement. A class can implement multiple interfaces.

sections.heading: Abstract Classes vs Interfaces
sections.content: Choosing between an abstract class and an interface depends on design needs.

rows.abstractClass: A class can inherit only one.

rows.interface: Defines what an object can do.

sections.heading: The super Keyword
sections.content: Use the super keyword to call a parent class implementation inside an overridden method.

sections.heading: Composition over Inheritance
sections.content: Modern Android development often prefers composition over inheritance to avoid deep hierarchies. Use inheritance when there is a true "is-a" relationship, and composition when there is a "has-a" relationship.

sections.heading: Summary
items: Classes are final by default; use open to allow inheritance.
Use override to redefine parent methods.
Interfaces allow shared behavior across unrelated classes.
Interfaces can have default method implementations but cannot store state.
Prefer composition over inheritance when appropriate.
